title: 10 best herbal creams for peripheral arterial occlusive disease

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maidenhair tree creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The creams' active ingredients, such as Ginkgo biloba extract, help to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the affected areas, thereby alleviating symptoms of claudication and cramping in the legs. Additionally, the creams' moisturizing properties can also help to soothe dry and irritated skin, providing relief from discomfort and promoting overall circulation health.
remedy_properties: Ginkgo biloba: Improves blood flow by increasing nitric oxide production in blood vessels.
Turmeric (Curcuma longa): Reduces inflammation and oxidative stress, which can contribute to PAOD development.
Boswellia serrata: Inhibits the production of inflammatory compounds, reducing swelling and discomfort associated with PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds like ginsenosides, which have vasodilatory properties, making them effective in improving blood flow to affected areas.
Barks: Containing flavonoids and terpenoids, which help reduce inflammation and improve circulation by relaxing blood vessels.
remedy_recipe: Weigh out 100g of high-quality beeswax and melt it in a double boiler for 10 minutes.
Add 50g of coconut oil and stir until fully incorporated and smooth after 5 minutes.
Mix in 20g of ginkgo biloba extract powder standardized to 24% flavonoids and 6% terpenes after 2 minutes.
Gradually add 10g of distilled water while stirring with a spatula for 8 minutes until desired consistency achieved.
Pour the mixture into sterilized glass jars and let it cool at room temperature for 30 minutes before storing.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: St John's wort creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because of its unique ability to increase blood flow and reduce inflammation in the affected areas. The cream's active compounds, hyperforin and hypericin, have potent vasodilatory properties that help relax constricted blood vessels, allowing for improved perfusion and oxygenation of the extremities. Additionally, St John's wort's anti-inflammatory effects help to reduce oxidative stress and tissue damage associated with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, promoting a more favorable healing environment and potential pain relief.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant activity: St. John's Wort's antioxidant properties help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in affected arteries, improving blood flow and reducing symptoms of PAOD.

Anti-inflammatory activity: The herb's anti-inflammatory compounds, such as hyperforin and hypericin, decrease inflammation in the affected areas, which can improve circulation and alleviate pain and discomfort associated with PAOD.

Vasodilatory effects: St. John's Wort's vasodilatory properties help to relax blood vessels, increasing blood flow and oxygenation to affected areas, thus reducing symptoms of PAOD such as cramping, fatigue, and coldness in the legs.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, the roots are valued for their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, making them an excellent component in topical creams for PAD patients.

Leaves: With their high content of hyperforin, a potent antioxidant, the leaves help to reduce inflammation and promote blood circulation in the affected areas, aiding in the treatment of Peripheral Arterial Occlusive Disease.
remedy_recipe: Harvest and dry st john's wort (hypericum perforatum) flowers in shaded area for 10-14 days to prevent loss of bioactive compounds.
Combine 20g of dried st john's wort flowers with 100ml of cold pressed olive oil in a dark glass jar, store in refrigerator for 4 weeks.
Strain the infused oil mixture through cheesecloth into another container, discard solids and let the oil cool to room temperature.
Add 10% beeswax by weight to the cooled oil mixture, heat gently until melted and mixed thoroughly, then pour into sterilized containers.
Allow the cream to set at room temperature for 30-60 minutes before using, store in refrigerator for up to 6 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Frankincense creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they promote blood flow to affected limbs. The anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties of frankincense reduce swelling and ease pain in the legs, ankles, and feet. Additionally, its ability to improve circulation and oxygenation helps to reduce the formation of blood clots and plaques that can further restrict blood flow. By addressing these underlying issues, frankincense creams may help alleviate symptoms associated with peripheral arterial occlusive disease, improving overall quality of life for those affected.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory: Frankincense cream's anti-inflammatory properties reduce swelling and pain in affected areas, improving circulation and relieving symptoms of PAOD.
Oxidative stress reduction: Frankincense's antioxidant properties help neutralize free radicals that contribute to oxidative stress, which can damage blood vessels and worsen PAOD symptoms.

Wound healing enhancement: Frankincense cream's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties promote wound healing by reducing bacteria growth, promoting tissue repair, and improving collagen synthesis, all of which are beneficial for treating ulcers and wounds associated with PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the frankincense plant contain high amounts of boswellic acid, which has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and swelling in patients with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of frankincense have been traditionally used to treat circulatory problems, including peripheral arterial occlusive disease, due to their ability to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Gather high-quality ingredients including 10% w/w boswellia serrata extract, coconut oil, beeswax, and sweet almond oil.
Melt 20g of beeswax in a double boiler or microwave-safe bowl for 30 seconds at intervals until fully liquefied.
Add 40g of melted coconut oil and stir well to combine, then add 10g of boswellia serrata extract and mix thoroughly.
Combine the mixture with 20g of sweet almond oil and stir until a smooth consistency is achieved, taking about 5 minutes.
Pour the mixture into sterilized containers and allow it to cool and solidify at room temperature for at least 30 minutes before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: White willow creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain salicin, a natural pain reliever that reduces inflammation and improves blood flow to the affected areas. By dilating the blood vessels, white willow cream can help increase oxygen and nutrient delivery to the legs, feet, and toes, reducing symptoms such as cramping, numbness, and weakness. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties can reduce swelling and pain associated with PAD, improving overall quality of life for individuals suffering from this condition.
remedy_properties: Salicin content: White willow cream contains salicin, a natural derivative of aspirin, which helps to reduce inflammation and improve blood flow to affected areas, thereby alleviating symptoms of PAOD.

Analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties: The herbal cream's analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties can help to relieve pain, swelling, and discomfort associated with PAOD, making it an effective alternative or adjunct treatment to traditional medications.
Vasodilatory effects: White willow extract has been shown to dilate blood vessels, which can help improve circulation and reduce the risk of further occlusion or blockage in affected arteries.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain salicin, a compound similar to aspirin, which helps to reduce inflammation and relieve pain in patients with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
Barks: The barks of the white willow tree contain salicin and other anti-inflammatory compounds that help to improve blood circulation and alleviate symptoms of peripheral artery disease.
remedy_recipe: Weigh 20 grams of dried salix alba bark and grind it into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Combine the powdered salix alba bark with 50 milliliters of distilled water in a glass beaker, stirring gently for 5 minutes to create a decoction.
Steep the mixture at room temperature for 30 minutes before straining it through a cheesecloth or coffee filter into a separate container, discarding the solids.
Add 10 grams of petroleum jelly and 20 milliliters of beeswax to the strained liquid, stirring until fully incorporated and melted, then cooling to around 60 degrees celsius for 15 minutes.
Pour the mixture into small glass jars or containers, allowing it to cool completely and harden at room temperature for several hours before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginseng creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because it has been shown to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the affected areas. The herbal properties of ginseng have been found to dilate blood vessels, increasing perfusion and reducing oxidative stress. This can help alleviate symptoms such as pain, cramping, and numbness associated with PAD. Additionally, ginseng's anti-inflammatory effects may also help reduce scarring and fibrosis in the affected vessels, promoting a healthier circulation and potentially slowing disease progression.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Ginseng creams contain antioxidants that help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the body, which can contribute to PAOD by damaging blood vessels and reducing blood flow.

Vasodilatory properties: The active compounds in ginseng creams can help dilate blood vessels, improving blood flow and oxygen delivery to the affected areas, thereby alleviating symptoms of PAOD such as pain and cramping.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Ginseng creams contain anti-inflammatory agents that help reduce inflammation in the affected arteries, which can contribute to PAOD by causing further damage and narrowing of blood vessels.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the ginseng plant are rich in bioactive compounds that help improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation, making them effective for treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of ginseng contain saponins, which have been shown to inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce oxidative stress, contributing to the prevention and treatment of peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_recipe: Prepare the herb by grinding 10 grams of dried panax ginseng root into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 5 minutes.
Mix the powdered ginseng with 20 grams of beeswax and 40 grams of coconut oil in a double boiler over low heat for 15 minutes.
Combine 100 milliliters of distilled water and 10 grams of glycerin in a separate saucepan and bring to a simmer over medium heat for 5 minutes.
Gradually add the ginseng-beeswax-coconut oil mixture to the water-glycerin mixture while stirring constantly for 2 minutes.
Remove the saucepan from the heat and let it cool to room temperature before pouring the cream into sterilized jars for storage at a controlled temperature of 15°c to 25°c.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Hawthorn creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain flavonoids, which have been shown to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the affected limbs. The extracts also exhibit vasodilatory properties, relaxing the blood vessels and increasing perfusion to areas previously compromised by blocked or narrowed arteries. Additionally, hawthorn's antioxidant effects help protect the endothelial lining of blood vessels from oxidative damage, further enhancing its benefits for patients with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties: Hawthorn's antioxidant compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in blood vessels, which can contribute to the development of PAOD.

Vasodilatory effects: Hawthorn's flavonoids and other bioactive compounds relax and dilate blood vessels, improving blood flow to the affected areas and helping to alleviate symptoms of PAOD such as pain, cramping, and discoloration.

Antithrombotic properties: Hawthorn's saponins and flavonoids have been shown to inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce thrombus formation, which can help prevent further blockages in blood vessels already affected by PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: Hawthorn roots are rich in flavonoids and triterpenoid saponins, which have vasodilatory properties that help improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the affected areas.
Barks: Hawthorn barks contain oligomeric proanthocyanidins (OPCs) and other polyphenols that have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, helping to soothe and protect the damaged blood vessels.
remedy_recipe: Harvest crataegus monogyna leaves and stems from organic sources in accordance with good manufacturing practices guidelines.
Dry the plant material in a low-temperature oven at 35°c for 48 hours or air-dry it for 7 days to preserve potency.
Combine 200g of dried crataegus monogyna material with 1l of cold-pressed olive oil and 20ml of glycerin in a glass container.
Allow the mixture to macerate at room temperature for 21 days, stirring daily to extract active compounds from the plant material.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean glass jar, discarding solids and storing the cream in an airtight container at 15°c for up to 6 months.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Purple coneflower creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because of its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The cream's active ingredient, Echinacea purpurea, has been shown to improve blood flow by reducing inflammation in the affected arteries, which can help alleviate symptoms such as pain, numbness, and cramping. Additionally, the cream's ability to scavenge free radicals helps protect against oxidative damage, promoting healthy vessel function and potentially slowing disease progression.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory activity: The cream's anti-inflammatory properties may help reduce inflammation and swelling in affected areas, improving blood flow and alleviating symptoms of PAOD.

Antioxidant effects: Antioxidants present in the purple coneflower cream can help neutralize free radicals that contribute to oxidative stress and damage to blood vessels, potentially slowing down the progression of PAOD.

Vasodilatory properties: The cream's vasodilatory properties may help relax blood vessel walls, improving blood flow and reducing symptoms such as pain, numbness, or weakness in the legs associated with PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the purple coneflower contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce inflammation and improve blood flow in patients with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
Seeds: Purple coneflower seeds have been shown to possess antioxidant properties, which can help protect against oxidative stress and damage caused by Peripheral Arterial Occlusive Disease (PAOD).
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried echinacea purpurea flowers at the peak of bloom for optimal potency.
Infuse 20 grams of echinacea purpurea in 200 milliliters of cold-pressed olive oil for 4 hours.
Strain and filter the infused oil mixture through cheesecloth to separate the herbal extract from solids.
Add 10% beeswax by weight and heat the mixture to 65°c while stirring until fully incorporated.
Pour the mixture into sterile containers and allow it to cool and solidify completely before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ginger creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they promote blood flow and circulation in the affected limbs. The anti-inflammatory properties of ginger relax constricted blood vessels, reducing pain and discomfort associated with blocked arteries. Additionally, ginger's antioxidant compounds help to reduce oxidative stress, which can contribute to plaque buildup in the arteries. By improving circulation and reducing inflammation, herbal ginger creams may alleviate symptoms such as numbness, tingling, and cramping, providing relief for individuals suffering from peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Antioxidant property: Ginger's antioxidant properties help protect the body from oxidative stress caused by free radicals, which can contribute to the development and progression of PAOD. By reducing oxidative stress, herbal ginger creams may help prevent further damage to blood vessels.

This vasodilation effect helps improve overall circulation and reduces the risk of complications.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 20g of fresh ginger root zingiber officinale and wash thoroughly with lukewarm water.
Dry the ginger in the shade or using a food dehydrator at 30°c for 24 hours.
Grind 10g of dried ginger into fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle.
Mix 50g of coconut oil, 20g of beeswax, and 5g of powdered ginger in a double boiler.
Stir the mixture over low heat for 30 minutes or until it reaches 60°c to create a smooth cream.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: English lavender creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they promote relaxation and reduce inflammation. The calming properties of lavender help to soothe the body, easing tension in the affected limbs and improving blood flow. remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties, making it effective in reducing swelling and pain associated with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality fresh or dried lavandula angustifolia flowers in the summer season weighing approximately 200g.
Combine the harvested lavender flowers with 100ml of carrier oil such as sweet almond oil or coconut oil in a double boiler.
Heat the mixture for 30 minutes at low temperature of around 60°c to 70°c allowing for efficient extraction.
Strain the infusion through cheesecloth into a clean glass container and discard the solids then add 100ml of distilled water.
Homogenize the liquid extract by blending it with 200g of emulsifier-based cream base and stabilizer for 2 hours to achieve smooth consistency.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Maypop creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they promote blood flow to affected areas by dilating constricted blood vessels. The cream's active compounds, including flavonoids and terpenes, stimulate vasodilation, increasing oxygen and nutrient delivery to the limbs. This improves symptoms such as intermittent claudication, cramping, and weakness, allowing individuals to engage in daily activities with greater ease and mobility. remedy_recipe: Harvest 20 grams of fresh passionflower leaves and stems from a pesticide-free environment.
Dry the harvested material in a shaded area at room temperature for 7-10 days or by using a food dehydrator on low heat for 6 hours.
Combine 200 milliliters of cold pressed coconut oil with the dried passiflora incarnata and heat it in a double boiler setup for 2 hours while stirring occasionally.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a clean container, discarding the solids and reserving the infused oil.

Whip 100 grams of beeswax with 100 milliliters of the prepared passiflora incarnata oil until it reaches a smooth consistency, allowing it to cool at room temperature for 30 minutes before filling into containers.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Ironwort creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain bioactive compounds that exhibit potent anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory properties. These compounds, found in high concentrations in the cream, help to reduce inflammation and promote blood flow in affected areas. By increasing blood circulation, ironwort creams can alleviate symptoms such as pain, cramping, and numbness in the legs, feet, and toes commonly associated with peripheral arterial occlusive disease. Antithrombotic effects: Ironwort creams may also help prevent blood clot formation by inhibiting platelet aggregation and thrombin activity, which can reduce the risk of thromboembolic events in patients with PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the ironwort plant contain bioactive compounds that help to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation, making them effective in treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of ironwort have been shown to possess anti-platelet properties, which can help to prevent blood clots from forming and reduce the risk of peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_recipe: Collect dried sideritis scardica flowers at the rate of 200g per 100ml of base cream and store them in an airtight container.
Combine 50ml of sweet almond oil with 20ml of beeswax and heat them gently over low heat for 30 minutes stirring occasionally until melted.
Add the dried sideritis scardica flowers to the warm oil mixture and stir well allowing it to steep for 24 hours.
Strain the infused mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter into another container discarding the solids and squeezing out as much liquid as possible.
Pour the filtered herbal infusion into sterilized glass jars filling them to within 1cm of the top and seal immediately allowing it to cool and set at room temperature for 24 hours before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Lemon balm creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they have natural anti-inflammatory properties that can help to reduce swelling and improve blood flow to affected areas. The cream's soothing and calming effects can also provide relief from pain, cramping, and numbness associated with PAD. Vasodilatory properties: Lemon balm's vasodilatory effects can help relax narrowed or constricted blood vessels, improving blood flow and reducing symptoms such as claudication (pain when walking) in PAOD patients.

remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality melissa officinalis leaves, ensuring they are free from contaminants and pesticides.
Dry the harvested leaves using a food dehydrator or oven on low heat for 2 hours.
Create a tincture by steeping 20 grams of dried melissa officinalis in 100ml of vodka for 14 days, shaking daily.
Combine 10% w/w of the tincture with 90% w/w of a skin-friendly cream base and heat to 60°c for 30 minutes.
Allow the mixture to cool, then fill and seal jars for storage at room temperature for up to 6 months, allowing it to mature for 2 weeks before use.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tea creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain a blend of herbs that work synergistically to improve blood flow, reduce inflammation, and relax blood vessels. The antioxidants and flavonoids present in these herbal teas help to protect against oxidative stress and damage to the endothelium, promoting healthy blood vessel function. Vasodilatory properties: Herbal teas containing compounds like caffeine (from green tea), theobromine (from cacao), or berberine (from Berberis aristata) can help dilate blood vessels, improving blood flow to affected areas and reducing symptoms of PAOD.

Anti-inflammatory properties: Herbal teas with anti-inflammatory compounds like ginger, turmeric, or rosemary may help reduce inflammation in the affected blood vessels, which can contribute to PAOD by promoting blood vessel constriction and reducing blood flow.

remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality green tea leaves of camellia sinensis and dry them thoroughly for optimal potency.
Grind dried green tea leaves into a fine powder using a spice grinder or mortar and pestle, measuring out 20 grams per batch.
Mix the ground green tea powder with 100 milliliters of cold-pressed olive oil and 10 milliliters of beeswax in a heat-resistant glass bowl, stirring until well combined.
Place the mixture in a double boiler or a makeshift one using a glass bowl over a pot of simmering water, heating it to 60°c for 30 minutes while stirring occasionally.
Remove the mixture from heat and let it cool and solidify at room temperature, which takes approximately 2 hours, before whipping it into a smooth and creamy consistency.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Asiatic pennywort creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because of its unique ability to improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation. Improves microcirculation: Asiatic Pennywort creams have been shown to improve microcirculation, which is essential for maintaining healthy blood flow in peripheral arteries. This can help reduce symptoms such as intermittent claudication, cramping, and numbness.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in bioactive compounds that help improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation in the affected areas.
Rhyzomes: Contain anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that aid in reducing pain and swelling associated with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_recipe: Harvest and dry 100g of fresh centella asiatica leaves, allowing for optimal extraction of bioactive compounds.
Grind the dried centella asiatica into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle, taking approximately 30 minutes.
Mix 20g of the ground centella asiatica powder with 50g of cold-pressed coconut oil in a glass beaker, allowing for a 4-hour infusion period.
Blend the infused mixture with 10g of beeswax, 5g of shea butter, and 2g of vitamin e oil using an immersion blender, taking approximately 5 minutes.
Pour the cream mixture into sterile glass containers, filling to the brim, and allowing it to cool and solidify at room temperature within 1 hour before storage.

remedy_recipe: Collect and dry 50 grams of fresh vaccinium myrtillus berries and grind them into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle.
Mix the powdered vaccinium myrtillus with 100 milliliters of cold-pressed olive oil in a glass jar, stirring until a smooth paste forms.
Add 20 grams of beeswax and 10 grams of coconut oil to the mixture, heating it at 65 degrees celsius for 30 minutes to create a consistent texture.

remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, oak roots help improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in PAOD.
Barks: Containing triterpenes and phenolic compounds, oak barks exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which alleviate symptoms of PAOD.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 50 grams of dried quercus robur bark and grind it into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle for 10 minutes.
Combine the powdered bark with 100 milliliters of olive oil in a double boiler, heating it gently over low heat for 30 minutes.
Stir in 20 grams of beeswax and 10 grams of coconut oil until fully incorporated, increasing the heat to medium for an additional 5 minutes.

Cardiovascular protective properties: Some studies suggest that Yew extracts may exhibit cardiovascular protective effects by reducing the risk of atherosclerosis (plaque buildup in arteries), which can contribute to PAOD.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain alkaloids like taxine, which helps to improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation, making it beneficial for treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
Barks: The barks have been traditionally used to treat circulatory disorders, including peripheral arterial occlusive disease, due to their anti-inflammatory and vasodilatory properties.
remedy_recipe: Gather dried leaves of taxus baccata and store them in an airtight container away from direct sunlight.
Combine one part dried taxus baccata leaves with two parts coconut oil and infuse the mixture for six hours under room temperature.
Strain the infused mixture through cheesecloth or a coffee filter to remove the plant material and transfer it to a clean glass jar.
Add 5% vitamin e oil and 2% beeswax to the mixture, stirring until the wax is fully incorporated and the mixture has reached a smooth consistency.
Allow the cream to cool and thicken for at least two hours before transferring it to airtight containers for storage.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Turmeric creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because they contain curcumin, a powerful anti-inflammatory compound that reduces inflammation and improves circulation. By reducing inflammation in affected arteries, turmeric cream may help alleviate symptoms such as pain, cramping, and weakness in the legs and feet. Additionally, curcumin's antioxidant properties help protect against oxidative stress and damage to blood vessels, promoting healthy vessel function and potentially slowing disease progression. This natural approach can complement traditional treatments and improve overall quality of life for individuals with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory property: Turmeric's active compound, curcumin, reduces inflammation in the affected areas, which helps to alleviate pain and discomfort associated with PAOD.
Antioxidant property: Turmeric's antioxidant properties help to neutralize free radicals that can damage blood vessels and worsen PAOD, promoting vascular health and preventing further damage.

Vasodilatory property: Turmeric's active compounds, including curcumin and other polyphenols, dilate blood vessels and improve circulation, which helps to reduce symptoms of PAOD such as claudication (pain with walking) and rest pain.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: The thick, underground stems of turmeric contain high amounts of curcuminoids, which have potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that can help alleviate symptoms of peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Roots: Turmeric roots are rich in flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to improve blood flow and reduce inflammation in the body, making them effective for treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality turmeric rhizomes of curcuma longa and wash them thoroughly with distilled water.
Grind the dried turmeric rhizomes into a fine powder using a spice grinder for 30 minutes.
Mix one part powdered turmeric with two parts coconut oil in a double boiler and heat gently for 1 hour.
Add beeswax and vitamin e oil to the mixture and stir until they melt completely in 20 minutes.
Pour the cream into sterilized glass jars and let it cool at room temperature before storing in an airtight container.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Field horsetail creams helps with peripheral arterial occlusive disease because of their unique ability to improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation. The herb's high concentration of silicates, flavonoids, and phenolic acids work together to relax constricted blood vessels and increase oxygenation to the affected limbs. Additionally, field horsetail's anti-inflammatory properties help to reduce pain and discomfort associated with PAD, allowing for improved mobility and quality of life.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities: Field Horsetail contains flavonoids and other polyphenols that have potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which can help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in affected blood vessels, thereby improving blood flow.

Vasodilatory properties: The cream's bioactive compounds, such as silica and saponins, can dilate blood vessels and improve blood circulation, allowing more oxygen and nutrients to reach affected areas, alleviating symptoms of PAOD.

Wound-healing and tissue repair promotion: Field Horsetail's extracts have been shown to enhance collagen synthesis, promote wound healing, and reduce the risk of complications in chronic wounds, which is particularly beneficial for patients with PAOD who may experience slow-healing wounds or ulcers.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in silicon and other minerals, Field Horsetail roots help improve blood circulation and reduce inflammation, making them ideal for treating peripheral arterial occlusive disease.

Rhyzomes: Containing bioactive compounds like flavonoids and phenolic acids, Field Horsetail rhyzomes have been shown to inhibit platelet aggregation and reduce oxidative stress, beneficial for patients with PAD.
remedy_recipe: Harvest high-quality equisetum arvense leaves and stems, ensuring they are free of contaminants and pesticides.
Dry the harvested plant material in a warm dark place for several hours or overnight.
Grind 20 grams of dried equisetum arvense into a fine powder using a mortar and pestle or coffee grinder.
Mix one teaspoon of the equisetum arvense powder with three tablespoons of cold-pressed coconut oil in a small saucepan over low heat for 10 minutes.
Strain the mixture through cheesecloth into a glass jar, pressing on the solids to extract as much cream as possible, and let it cool before storage.

intro_desc: Herbal creams for peripheral arterial occlusive disease are topical preparations that combine natural plant extracts to promote blood flow, reduce inflammation, and improve circulation in affected limbs. These creams help alleviate symptoms such as pain, numbness, and fatigue associated with PAD by stimulating the body's natural healing processes. Examples of herbal creams that benefit patients with PAD include those containing Ginkgo biloba, turmeric, and capsaicin, which have been shown to increase blood flow and reduce pain. By using these creams, individuals with PAD can experience improved mobility, reduced discomfort, and enhanced overall quality of life.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat peripheral arterial occlusive disease with herbal creams is by using a combination of Ginkgo biloba, Butcher's Broom, and Horse Chestnut. These herbs help improve blood flow, reduce inflammation, and strengthen the walls of affected blood vessels. Apply topical creams containing these ingredients to the affected areas twice daily for 3-4 weeks, or as directed by a healthcare professional. This natural treatment can effectively alleviate symptoms such as pain, cramping, and fatigue in patients with peripheral arterial occlusive disease.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to peripheral arterial occlusive disease that are treated with herbal creams are chronic venous insufficiency, varicose veins, and Raynaud's disease. Herbal creams containing ingredients like horse chestnut extract, bilberry extract, and capsaicin have been used to alleviate symptoms such as pain, swelling, and discoloration associated with these conditions. These creams may help improve circulation, reduce inflammation, and promote healing.
